mnemonics.title: INT Instruction Sequence
mnemonics.content: When INT executes: FLAGS → CS → IP. Remember as 'First Save Flags, Then Code Segment, Then Instruction Pointer' (F-S-C-I). The last pushed is first popped by IRET.

mnemonics.title: IRET Restores in Reverse
mnemonics.content: IRET pops in reverse order: IP ← CS ← FLAGS. Think of it as 'In Reverse Pop Order' (IRP) - whatever INT pushed last, IRET pops first.

mnemonics.title: INT vs INTO
mnemonics.content: INT always fires (unconditional), INTO checks Overflow (conditional). Remember: INTerrupt = INTermittent always; INTO = INTermittent only if Overflow.

acronyms.usage: 256-entry table at 0:0 containing CS:IP addresses of ISR handlers
Special subroutine executed in response to an interrupt
Instruction to return from ISR and restore processor state
Instruction to generate a software interrupt with type number n
Conditional software interrupt triggered if OF=1
Flag that enables/disables maskable hardware interrupts

visualTips.visualization: Draw a stack growing downward. Show FLAGS pushed first (lowest), then CS, then IP on top. When IRET executes, IP pops first (top), then CS, then FLAGS.
Draw 256 boxes in a row at address 0:0. Each box is 4 bytes (2 for IP, 2 for CS). Box 0 at 0-3, Box 1 at 4-7, Box n at (n×4) to (n×4+3).
Draw flowchart: Execute INT n → Push FLAGS → Push CS → Push IP → Clear TF, IF → Load CS:IP from IVT[n×4] → Execute ISR.

analogies.analogy: Like calling a manager (INT instruction) to handle a special request within a company. The normal work (main program) pauses, the manager handles the issue (ISR), then returns to normal work (IRET).
The Interrupt Vector Table is like a company's emergency phonebook. Each emergency type (0-255) has a specific phone number (CS:IP address) to call the right person (ISR).
Software interrupt is like deciding to take a coffee break yourself (self-initiated). Hardware interrupt is like a phone ringing (externally triggered) - you must answer it.
